subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.
List - IList - II
A. Boltzmann constantI. $[M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$
B. Stefan's constantII. $[ML^2T^{-1}]$
C. Planck's constantIII. $[ML^2T^{-2}K^{-1}]$
D. Gravitational constantIV. $[ML^0T^{-3}K^{-4}]$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List - I with List - II.
List - IList - II
A. Boltzmann constantI. $[M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$
B. Stefan's constantII. $[ML^2T^{-1}]$
C. Planck's constantIII. $[ML^2T^{-2}K^{-1}]$
D. Gravitational constantIV. $[ML^0T^{-3}K^{-4}]$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Boltzmann constant $k$: $E = k T \Rightarrow [k] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[K]} = [ML^2T^{-2}K^{-1}]$

Stefan's constant $\sigma$: $\dfrac{E}{At} = \sigma T^4 \Rightarrow [\sigma] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[L^2][T][K^4]} = [ML^0T^{-3}K^{-4}]$

Planck's constant $h$: $E = h\nu \Rightarrow [h] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[T^{-1}]} = [ML^2T^{-1}]$

Gravitational constant $G$: $F = \dfrac{Gm_1m_2}{r^2} \Rightarrow [G] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}][L^2]}{[M^2]} = [M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$

Thus, A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I

subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List - I with List - II.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
(A) $[\mathrm{Y}]=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{A}\left(\frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell}\right)} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}}{\mathrm{~L}^2}=\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}$
(B) Torque $(\vec{\tau})=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$
$(\vec{\tau})=\mathrm{L} \times \mathrm{MLT}^{-2}=\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}(\mathrm{IV})$
(C) Coefficient of viscosity $\Rightarrow F=\eta A \frac{d V}{d t}$
$\eta \rightarrow \mathrm{Pa} \cdot \mathrm{sec}$
$[\eta]=\frac{\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}}{\mathrm{~L}^2} \times \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{-1}$
(D) Gravitational constant (G)
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}_1 \mathrm{M}_2}{\mathrm{r}^2} \\
& {[\mathrm{G}]=\frac{\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{r}^2}{\mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{MLT}^{-2} \times \mathrm{L}^2}{\mathrm{M}^2}=\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}}
\end{aligned}$

q: The dimensional formula of latent heat is :
question: The dimensional formula of latent heat is :
solution:
Latent heat is specific heat
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}}{\mathrm{M}}=\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}$

question:
Match List I with List II

  List I   List II
A Angular momentum I ML2T-2
B Torque II ML-2T-2
C Stress III ML2T-1
D Pressure gradient IV ML-1T-2

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
As we know, angular momentum is given by, L→=r→×p→⇒L=M0 L1 T0M1 L1 T-1

=M1 L2 T-1

Torque is given by, 

τ→=r→×F→⇒τ=L1MLT-2

=ML2 T-2

Also, Stress ≡ Pressure =FA

Hence, [Stress] =ML-1 T-2

Pressure Gradient =dPdx 

Therefore, [Pressure Gradient]=ML-2 T-2

q: If momentum<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>)</mo></math> are taken to be the fundamental quantities then the dimensional formula for energy is :
question: If momentum<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>)</mo></math> are taken to be the fundamental quantities then the dimensional formula for energy is :
solution:
Let dimension formula of energy will be

E=AaTbPc
P=M1L1T-1
A=L2
T=T1
M1L2T-2=MCL2a+cTb-c
by comparison C=1.........1
2a+c=2......2
b-c=-2........3
c=1,  a=1/2,b=-1
E=A1/2T-1P1

question: The electric flux is $\phi=\alpha \sigma+\beta \lambda$ where $\lambda$ and $\sigma$ are linear and surface charge density, respectively. $\left(\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)$ represents

B Co-efficient of Viscosity (η) II [ML2 T –1]
C Planck's Constant (h) III [ML–1 T –2]
D Work Function ϕ IV [ML2 T–2]

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) Young's modulus is stated asY=StressStrain=FA∆ll=FlA∆l=MLT-2LL2L=ML-1T-2(B) Coefficient of viscosity is stated as η=F6πrv, here, v is velocity.

η=MLT-2LLT-1=ML-1T-1

(C) Planck's Constant is stated as h=EnergyFrequency=ML2T-2T-1=ML2T-1

(D) Work function has the same dimension as that of energy, so ϕ=ML2T-2

Thus, A-III, B-I, C-II, D-IV

subject: Physics
q: The dimensions of angular momentum, latent heat and capacitance are, respectively.
question: The dimensions of angular momentum, latent heat and capacitance are, respectively.
solution:
Angular momentum $=\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{v} \times \mathrm{r}$
$$
=\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}
$$
Latent heat $\mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}}{\mathrm{M}}=\mathrm{L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}$
Capacitance $\mathrm{C}=\frac{\text { Charge }}{\text { P.d. }}=\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^{-2} \mathrm{~T}^4 \mathrm{~A}^2$

subject: Physics
q: Keeping the significant figures in view, the sum of the physical quantities $52.01 \mathrm{~m}, 153.2 \mathrm{~m}$ and 0.123 m is :
question: Keeping the significant figures in view, the sum of the physical quantities $52.01 \mathrm{~m}, 153.2 \mathrm{~m}$ and 0.123 m is :
solution:
When adding measurements, the result must be limited to the least number of decimal places among all quantities.
The measurements are: 52.01 m (2 decimal places), 153.2 m (1 decimal place), and 0.123 m (3 decimal places).
The limiting factor is 1 decimal place.
Calculating the sum: 52.01 + 153.2 + 0.123 = 205.333 m.
Rounding to 1 decimal place gives 205.3 m, which is option (1).

subject: Physics
q: $L$, $C$ and $R$ represents physical quantities inductance, capacitance and resistance respectively. The dimensional formula $ML^2 T^{-4} A^{-2}$ corresponds to __________.
question: $L$, $C$ and $R$ represents physical quantities inductance, capacitance and resistance respectively. The dimensional formula $ML^2 T^{-4} A^{-2}$ corresponds to __________.
solution:
Dimensional formula of inductance $L$ is obtained from $E = \dfrac{1}{2}LI^2$:

$[L] = \dfrac{[E]}{[I]^2} = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-2}}{A^2} = ML^2T^{-2}A^{-2}$

Dimensional formula of capacitance $C$ is obtained from $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{Q^2}{C}$:

$[C] = \dfrac{[Q]^2}{[E]} = \dfrac{A^2T^2}{ML^2T^{-2}} = M^{-1}L^{-2}T^4A^2$

Dimensional formula of resistance $R$ is obtained from $P = I^2R$:

$[R] = \dfrac{[P]}{[I]^2} = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-3}}{A^2} = ML^2T^{-3}A^{-2}$

The dimensional formula of $\sqrt{LC}$ is:

$[\sqrt{LC}] = \sqrt{(ML^2T^{-2}A^{-2})(M^{-1}L^{-2}T^4A^2)} = \sqrt{T^2} = T$

Evaluating the dimensional formula of $\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{LC}}$:

$\left[\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{LC}}\right] = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-3}A^{-2}}{T} = ML^2T^{-4}A^{-2}$

This matches the given dimensional formula.

Answer: $\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{LC}}$

subject: Physics
q:
Consider a modified Bernoulli equation.
$\left(\mathrm{P}+\frac{A}{B t^{2}}\right)+\rho g(h+B t)+\frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2}=$ constant
If $t$ has the dimension of time then the dimensions of $A$ and $B$ are $\_\_\_\_$, $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
question:
Consider a modified Bernoulli equation.
$\left(\mathrm{P}+\frac{A}{B t^{2}}\right)+\rho g(h+B t)+\frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2}=$ constant
If $t$ has the dimension of time then the dimensions of $A$ and $B$ are $\_\_\_\_$, $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
solution:
All terms must have dimension of pressure $[ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$.
For $(h + Bt)$: Since $\rho gh$ has dimension of pressure, $[h] = [L]$.
For dimensional consistency: $[Bt] = [L]$, so $[B] = [LT^{-1}] = [M^0LT^{-1}]$
For $\frac{A}{Bt^2}$: $\frac{[A]}{[B][T^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$
$[A] = [B][T^2][ML^{-1}T^{-2}] = [LT^{-1}][T^2][ML^{-1}T^{-2}] = [ML^0T^{-1}]$
$[A] = [ML^0T^{-1}]$ and $[B] = [M^0LT^{-1}]$

subject: Physics
q: The potential energy of a particle changes with distance $x$ from a fixed origin as $V = \dfrac{A\sqrt{x}}{x + B}$, where $A$ and $B$ are constant with appropriate dimensions. The dimensions of $AB$ are _______.
question: The potential energy of a particle changes with distance $x$ from a fixed origin as $V = \dfrac{A\sqrt{x}}{x + B}$, where $A$ and $B$ are constant with appropriate dimensions. The dimensions of $AB$ are _______.
solution:
By the principle of dimensional homogeneity, quantities added or subtracted must have the same dimensions. In the denominator, $B$ is added to $x$ (distance).

$[B] = [x] = [L]$

The dimensions of potential energy $V$ are $[M^1 L^2 T^{-2}]$.

From the given equation $V = \dfrac{A\sqrt{x}}{x + B}$, we can write the dimensional formula as:

$[V] = \dfrac{[A][x]^{1/2}}{[x + B]}$

$[M^1 L^2 T^{-2}] = \dfrac{[A][L]^{1/2}}{[L]}$

$[M^1 L^2 T^{-2}] = [A][L]^{-1/2}$

$[A] = [M^1 L^2 T^{-2}][L]^{1/2} = [M^1 L^{5/2} T^{-2}]$

Now, the dimensions of $AB$ are:

$[AB] = [A][B] = [M^1 L^{5/2} T^{-2}][L] = [M^1 L^{7/2} T^{-2}]$

Answer: $[M^1 L^{7/2} T^{-2}]$

subject: Physics
q: In the equation X+aY2[Y-b]=RT, X is pressure, Y is volume, R is universal gas constant and T is temperature. The physical quantity equivalent to the ratio ab is:
question: In the equation X+aY2[Y-b]=RT, X is pressure, Y is volume, R is universal gas constant and T is temperature. The physical quantity equivalent to the ratio ab is:
solution:
X & aY2 have the same dimensions. Y & b also have the same dimensions.

Therefore,

aY2=ML-1T-2⇒a=ML-1T-2×L6=ML5T-2

and b=L3.

So, the dimensions of ab is,

ab=ML5T-2L3=ML2T-2.

This is the dimensional form of energy. Hence, this is the right option.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is not a dimensionless quantity?
question: Which of the following is not a dimensionless quantity?
solution: The power factor is defined as,<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mi>ϕ</mi></math> which is dimensionless, the formula of quality factor, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>R</mi></mfrac><msqrt><mfrac><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></mfrac></msqrt></math>, which is also dimensionless, and the permeability in free space, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>r</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>μ</mi><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfrac></math>, but permeability in free space is defined as,  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfrac></math>, here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is the speed of light in vacuum and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is the permittivity in free space so this having dimension.

question: Which one of the following is the correct dimensional formula for the capacitance in F ? $\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{L}, \mathrm{T}$ and C stand for unit of mass, length, time and charge,

The dimension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is
question: Consider two physical quantities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> related to each other as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>B</mi><mo>−</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> have dimensions of energy, length and time respectively. solution:

And dimensions of A=x2tE=L2TML2T−2=1MT−1

subject: Physics
q:
The equation of a circle is given by x2+y2=a2, where a is the radius. If the equation is modified to change the origin other than (0, 0), then find out the correct dimensions of A and B in a new equation :

x-At2+y-tB2=a2

The dimensions of t is given as T-1
question:
The equation of a circle is given by x2+y2=a2, where a is the radius. If the equation is modified to change the origin other than (0, 0), then find out the correct dimensions of A and B in a new equation :

x-At2+y-tB2=a2

The dimensions of t is given as T-1
solution:
Principle of Homogeneity states that dimensions of each of the terms of a dimensional equation on both sides should be the same. Then,

x-At2+y-tB2=a2

For first term, 

At=x⇒A×1T=L

∴A=T1L1

For the second term,

tB=y

⇒1TB=L

⇒B=T-1L-1

subject: Physics
q:
Applying the principle of homogeneity of dimensions, determine which one is correct,
where $T$ is time period, $G$ is gravitational constant, $M$ is mass, $r$ is radius of orbit.
question:
Applying the principle of homogeneity of dimensions, determine which one is correct,
where $T$ is time period, $G$ is gravitational constant, $M$ is mass, $r$ is radius of orbit.
solution:
According to principle of homogeneity dimension of LHS should be equal to dimensions of RHS so option (3) is correct.
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T}^2=\frac{4 \pi^2 \mathrm{r}^3}{\mathrm{GM}} \\
& {\left[\mathrm{T}^2\right]=\frac{\left[\mathrm{L}^3\right]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right][\mathrm{M}]}}
\end{aligned}$
(Dimension of $\mathrm{G}$ is $\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$ )
$\left[\mathrm{T}^2\right]=\frac{\left[\mathrm{L}^3\right]}{\left[\mathrm{L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]}=\left[\mathrm{T}^2\right]$

(A) Mass density $=\frac{M}{V}=M^1 L^{-3}$...(iv)

(C) Power $=\mathrm{F}. \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-3}$....(i)
(D) Moment of inertia $=\mathrm{Mr}^2=\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^2$...(iii)

B. Second (S)II. $\sqrt{\dfrac{Gh}{c^5}}$
C. Kilogram (M)III. $\sqrt{\dfrac{K^2L^2c^3}{Gh}}$
D. Kelvin (K)IV. $\sqrt{\dfrac{Gh}{c^3}}$

where $h$ (Planck's constant), $G$ (gravitational constant) and $c$ (speed of light in vacuum) as fundamental units.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-IList-II
A. Meter (L)I. $\sqrt{\dfrac{hc}{G}}$
B. Second (S)II. $\sqrt{\dfrac{Gh}{c^5}}$
C. Kilogram (M)III. $\sqrt{\dfrac{K^2L^2c^3}{Gh}}$
D. Kelvin (K)IV. $\sqrt{\dfrac{Gh}{c^3}}$

where $h$ (Planck's constant), $G$ (gravitational constant) and $c$ (speed of light in vacuum) as fundamental units.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
The dimensional formulas for the given fundamental constants are:
$[h] = M L^2 T^{-1}$
$[c] = L T^{-1}$
$[G] = M^{-1} L^3 T^{-2}$

Evaluating the dimensions of the expressions in List-II:

For I: $\sqrt{\dfrac{hc}{G}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(M L^2 T^{-1})(L T^{-1})}{M^{-1} L^3 T^{-2}}} = \sqrt{M^2} = M$ (Kilogram).
Thus, C $\rightarrow$ I.

For II: $\sqrt{\dfrac{Gh}{c^5}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(M^{-1} L^3 T^{-2})(M L^2 T^{-1})}{(L T^{-1})^5}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L^5 T^{-3}}{L^5 T^{-5}}} = \sqrt{T^2} = T$ (Second).
Thus, B $\rightarrow$ II.

For IV: $\sqrt{\dfrac{Gh}{c^3}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(M^{-1} L^3 T^{-2})(M L^2 T^{-1})}{(L T^{-1})^3}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L^5 T^{-3}}{L^3 T^{-3}}} = \sqrt{L^2} = L$ (Meter).
Thus, A $\rightarrow$ IV.

For III: Substituting $L^2 = \dfrac{Gh}{c^3}$ from IV into the expression gives $\sqrt{\dfrac{K^2 L^2 c^3}{Gh}} = \sqrt{K^2 \left(\dfrac{Gh}{c^3}\right) \dfrac{c^3}{Gh}} = \sqrt{K^2} = K$ (Kelvin).
Thus, D $\rightarrow$ III.

The correct matching is A-IV, B-II, C-I, D-III.

Answer: A-IV, B-II, C-I, D-III

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> is the electronic charge, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is the speed of light in free space and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is Planck's constant, the quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac><mfrac><msup><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mrow><mi>h</mi><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> has dimensions of :

subject: Physics
q: The dimension of stopping potential <math><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> in photoelectric effect in units of Planck’s constant ‘ <math><mi>h</mi></math> ’, speed of light ‘ <math><mi>c</mi></math> ’ and Gravitational constant ‘ <math><mi>G</mi></math> ’ and ampere <math><mi>A</mi></math> is:
question: The dimension of stopping potential <math><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> in photoelectric effect in units of Planck’s constant ‘ <math><mi>h</mi></math> ’, speed of light ‘ <math><mi>c</mi></math> ’ and Gravitational constant ‘ <math><mi>G</mi></math> ’ and ampere <math><mi>A</mi></math> is:
solution:
V=K(h)a(I)b(G)c(C)d (V is voltage)
We know [h]=ML2T-1
[I]=A
[G]=M-1L3T-2
[C]=LT-1
[V]=ML2T-3A-1
ML2T-3A-1=(ML2T-1)a(A)b(M-1L3T-2)c(LT-1)d
ML2T-3A-1=Ma-cL2a+3c+dT-a-2c-dAb
a-c=1 .....(1)
2a+3c+d=2 .....(2)
-a-2c-d=-3 .....(3)
b=-1  ...(4)
On solving
c=-1
a=0
d=5, b=-1
V=K(h)0(A)-1(G)-1(C)5

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following physical quantities have the same dimensions?
question: Which of the following physical quantities have the same dimensions?
options: Electric displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mfenced></math> and surface charge density
Current density and surface charge density
solution:
Electric displacement

D→=ϵ0E→

D=ϵ0E=ϵ0σϵ0

D=σ

And surface charge density =σ.

Thus, electric displacement D and surface charge density have same dimension.

subject: Physics
q: If velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>T</mi></mfenced></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>F</mi></mfenced></math> are chosen as the base quantities, the dimensions of the mass will be :
question: If velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>T</mi></mfenced></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>F</mi></mfenced></math> are chosen as the base quantities, the dimensions of the mass will be :
solution:
[M]1=[V]a[T]b[F]c
[M]1=LT-1a[T]bMLT-2c
[M]1=[M]c[L]a+c[T]-a+b-2c
Thus,

c=1,a+c=0 andb-a-2c=0⇒a=-1⇒b=-1+2=1
Thus, [M]=[V]-1[ T]1[ F]1
